title: Choosing the Right Application Architecture
concept: Selecting the right vision application means matching the task's real question — identify, locate, track, compare, flag, measure, or reconstruct — to the specific technique built for that exact question.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does combining face recognition and anomaly detection in one hospital camera system best illustrate?

options.text: Multiple specialized Phase 10 techniques can be combined to solve complementary sub-problems in one real system
Only one vision technique is ever allowed per organization
Face recognition and anomaly detection are identical techniques

exercises.explain: Yes! Real systems often compose multiple specialized tools together.

cards.title: So many tools — which one do I actually need?
cards.body: With thirteen different specialized applications now in your toolbox, the real skill isn't memorizing each one — it's quickly recognizing WHICH tool actually answers the question a real project is asking.

cards.title: Matching the Question to the Technique
cards.body: Each Phase 10 application answers a distinct underlying question: 'who is this?' (face recognition), 'where is text?' (OCR), 'what looks similar?' (retrieval), 'is this normal?' (anomaly detection), 'how far is this?' (depth), 'what's the full 3D shape?' (3D vision) — picking the right tool starts with correctly identifying the real question.

cards.title: A doctor picking the right diagnostic test
cards.body: A doctor doesn't run every possible medical test on every patient — they listen to the symptoms first, then choose the specific test (blood test, X-ray, MRI) that actually answers the real diagnostic question. Choosing a vision architecture works the same way: understand the real question before picking the tool.

cards.title: A retail 'find similar shoes' feature
cards.body: A shoe retailer wants users to photograph a shoe and find similar ones in stock. The underlying question is 'what looks similar to this?' — pointing directly to image retrieval (embeddings + nearest neighbor search), not object detection or classification, which answer different questions entirely.

cards.title: The fanciest technique isn't automatically the right one
cards.body: It's tempting to reach for the most advanced or exciting technique (like a full NeRF 3D reconstruction) even when a simpler tool (like basic depth estimation) would fully solve the actual problem. Matching complexity to genuine need avoids wasted engineering effort and unnecessary compute cost.

left.text:
'Who is this person?'
'What looks similar to this?'
'Is this abnormal?'

exercises.prompt: What is the first step in choosing the right vision application architecture for a project?

options.text: Correctly identifying the real underlying question the project is actually asking
Always picking the most complex, cutting-edge technique available
Randomly selecting from the thirteen Phase 10 applications
Skipping straight to model training without any planning

exercises.explain: Correct! Matching the real question to the right technique is the essential first step.

exercises.prompt: True or false: The most advanced or complex technique available is always the correct choice, regardless of what the actual problem requires.
exercises.explain: False. Matching technique complexity to genuine need avoids wasted engineering effort; simpler tools are often the right choice.

exercises.sentence: A shoe retailer's 'find similar shoes' feature points directly to image ___ rather than classification or detection.
exercises.explain: The underlying 'what looks similar' question is exactly what image retrieval answers.

exercises.prompt: Why is a doctor choosing a specific diagnostic test based on symptoms a good analogy for selecting a vision architecture?

options.text: Both involve identifying the real underlying question first, then choosing the specific tool built to answer exactly that question
Doctors and vision engineers use identical tools for every task
Medical tests have no relationship to selecting technical tools

exercises.explain: Correct! Both processes start with correctly diagnosing the real question before selecting a solution.

exercises.prompt: A startup wants to build a feature that reads text from photographed restaurant menus and translates it. Which Phase 10 technique's underlying question most directly matches 'where is the text and what does it say'?

options.text: OCR (Optical Character Recognition), since it directly answers the question of locating and reading text in images
Neural Style Transfer, since it focuses on artistic color and texture, not text
Multi-Object Tracking, since it focuses on following objects across video frames, not reading text
3D Vision, since it focuses on spatial geometry, not text content

exercises.explain: Exactly! OCR is purpose-built for exactly this question. Next: how multiple techniques get combined into real production systems.
